name: Waiotahi Beach
access_notes: SH2 roadside; Ōpōtiki SLSC and public toilets at east end; parking on beach reserve
snippet: Ōpōtiki's most popular beach — patrolled in summer, with pohutukawa-lined dunes, parking and picnic areas on the Bay of Plenty coast.

name: Tirohanga Beach
access_notes: Holiday park at access point 6 km east of Ōpōtiki on SH35; gentle sloping white sand
snippet: Safe, sheltered white-sand bay 6 km east of Ōpōtiki; part of the Motu Trails dune network; ideal for families.

name: Waioeka Beach / Ōpōtiki Spit
access_notes: Sandy spit at Ōpōtiki near Waioeka River mouth; town access
snippet: Low sandy spit at the mouth of the Waioeka River in central Ōpōtiki — casual access and good for surf fishing.

name: Te Waiti Nature Trail
access_notes: Turn off SH2 south of Ōpōtiki onto Otara Rd then Te Waiti Rd; well signposted
snippet: Short nature trail through nikau palms and tawa forest in the Urutawa Conservation Area — good birdlife, 14 km south of Ōpōtiki.

name: Boulders Campsite
access_notes: 4WD only due to storm damage on access track; steep rough descent to site; no dogs
snippet: Picturesque streamside camp next to Te Waiti Stream, surrounded by nikau and tawa — 4WD access only due to sustained storm damage.

name: Hukutaia Domain / Sacred Pūriri Tree
access_notes: Woodlands Rd off SH2 near Ōpōtiki; 7 km from town; domain reserve with car park
snippet: One of NZ's oldest living trees — a 2500-year-old pūriri held sacred by local Māori, set within a sheltered domain reserve near Ōpōtiki.

name: Waihau Bay Beach
access_notes: SH35 roadside access; store, boat ramp and holiday park at bay; 107 km from Ōpōtiki
snippet: Rock-strewn bay with sandy sections on the northern East Cape — a surf fishing and boat-launch hub with a store and accommodation.

name: Oruaiti Beach
access_notes: Short walk over private land from SH35 near Cape Runaway; no formal car park
snippet: Widely regarded as the best swimming beach on the western side of East Cape — safe, sandy and uncrowded, just before Cape Runaway.

name: Whangaparāoa / Cape Runaway
access_notes: Road access to headland from SH35; reinstated pā and meeting house open to visitors
snippet: Easternmost point of the Bay of Plenty — traditionally where the Tainui and Arawa waka landed; dramatic coastal headland with significant Māori heritage.

name: Lottin Point / Maraehako Bay
access_notes: Rough unsealed access road off SH35; motel at road end; no formal car park
snippet: Remote headland bay midway along the northern East Cape coast — known for exceptionally clear water and excellent surf casting.

name: Wharekahika / Hicks Bay Beach
access_notes: SH35 access to sheltered bay; motel and basic services at settlement
snippet: Sheltered bay on the dramatic eastern turn of the Cape — named for Cook's second-in-command; sudden shift in coastal character from the rugged western side.

name: Wharekahika / Hicks Bay Wharf
access_notes: Adjacent to beach; accessible on foot from SH35
snippet: Remnant of the coastal trading wharves that were the only link to the outside world before the East Cape road was completed in the 1930s.

name: East Cape / Te Ara Poutama
access_notes: 21 km mostly unsealed road from Te Araroa; car park at lighthouse base
snippet: Easternmost point of mainland New Zealand — the first place on earth to see each new day; 800-step climb to the lighthouse with sweeping Pacific views.

name: East Cape Lighthouse
access_notes: Car park at road end; ~800 steps up to lighthouse; allow 30 min each way
snippet: Working lighthouse at New Zealand's easternmost point — steep climb rewarded by panoramic views over the Pacific and back along the rugged East Cape coast.

name: Te-Waha-o-Rerekohu (Giant Pōhutukawa)
access_notes: Roadside in Te Araroa township; informal parking nearby
snippet: New Zealand's largest pōhutukawa — over 350 years old, 20 m tall and 40 m wide, and spectacular in summer bloom; in Te Araroa township.

name: Onepoto Bay
access_notes: Accessible from Hicks Bay area; sheltered shallow arc
snippet: Shallow, sheltered bay a short distance from Hicks Bay — a quieter alternative for a swim or short beach walk.

name: Tikitiki Anglican Church (St Mary's)
access_notes: On SH35 in Tikitiki township; roadside parking
snippet: Extraordinary 1924 Māori Anglican church built as a memorial to Ngāti Porou soldiers of WWI — one of the most ornately carved interiors of any church in New Zealand.

name: Waipiro Bay Beach
access_notes: 6 km off SH35 near Ruatoria on a rough unsealed road; basic facilities at settlement
snippet: Isolated bay with a long sandy beach — once a busy settlement with hotels and a maternity hospital; now quiet and rarely visited, with one of the region's finest sunrises.

name: Anaura Bay Walkway
access_notes: Turn off SH35 onto Anaura Rd, 67 km north of Gisborne; 8.7 km to campsite and trailhead
tripideas_article: Yes — Anaura Bay article covers this track
snippet: 3.5 km loop from the campsite through coastal bush with views over the bay — passes the 1769 Cook landing plaque; one of the most scenic easy walks on the Cape.

name: Anaura Bay DOC Campsite
access_notes: Booking required; no flush toilets — chemical toilet required; rubbish collected over Christmas/New Year only
tripideas_article: Yes — Anaura Bay article covers this
snippet: Beachside DOC campsite beside one of the most beautiful bays on the East Cape — flanked by coastal bush and stream; popular over summer.

name: Cooks Cove Walkway
access_notes: Small car park off the road to Tolaga Bay Wharf; 5 km return; 1.5–2 hrs
tripideas_article: Yes — Tolaga Bay article covers the wharf
snippet: 5 km coastal walk from Tolaga Bay to the sheltered cove where Cook anchored the Endeavour in 1769 — sea arch, waterfall, and interpretive panels along the route.

name: Te Tapuwae o Rongokako Marine Reserve
access_notes: Multiple road access points between Tolaga Bay and Gisborne off SH35
snippet: Protected marine reserve between Tolaga Bay and Gisborne — good snorkelling over rocky reef; diverse intertidal life accessible from several roadside access points.

name: Tatapouri Beach
access_notes: 532 Whangara Rd SH35, 15 min north of Gisborne; Dive Tatapouri and holiday park at access point
snippet: Reef beach 15 minutes north of Gisborne — home to wild short-tail stingray and eagle ray encounters at low tide through Dive Tatapouri; within Te Tapuwae o Rongokako Marine Reserve.

name: Wainui Beach
access_notes: SH35 through Wainui settlement; multiple road-end access points along beach
snippet: Gisborne's premier surf beach — 8 km east of the city, east-facing with consistent swell; popular with surfers and families; settlement with accommodation.

name: Okitu / Makorori Beach
access_notes: SH35 access; car park at beach with access to Makorori Point lookout track
snippet: Surf beach with sheltered swimming spots and rock pools, just north of Wainui — the Makorori Headland walk departs from here with views south to Young Nick's Head.

name: Okitu Bush Scenic Reserve
access_notes: Opposite Makorori Point on SH35; 20-min loop track; roadside parking
snippet: Small remnant of coastal broadleaf forest in an otherwise cleared landscape — 20-minute loop with birdlife and a lookout above the surf coast.

name: Makorori Point Lookout
access_notes: Short walk from Okitu Beach car park; exposed clifftop
snippet: Clifftop lookout above the surf coast between Makorori and Wainui beaches — clear day views south to Young Nick's Head and the Mahia Peninsula.

name: Whitikau Conservation Campsite
access_notes: Follow SH2 north from Gisborne to Matawai; Motu Rd to Toatoa; then Takaputahi Rd; gravel access; small campervans only
snippet: Remote Raukūmara Conservation Park campsite — 122 km north of Gisborne via inland Motu Road; gravel access; very basic; suited to self-sufficient campers.

name: Titirangi Reserve / Kaiti Hill
access_notes: Drive via Titirangi Drive or walk from Puhi Kai Iti; multiple track access points; car park near summit
snippet: 35 ha ancestral reserve rising above Gisborne to 140 m — 14 recorded archaeological sites; panoramic views over Poverty Bay; deep Cook and Ngāti Oneone significance.

name: Kaiti Beach
access_notes: Kaiti Beach Rd from central Gisborne; walking distance from Cook Landing Reserve
snippet: Historic beach on the eastern shore of the Tūranganui River mouth — landing site of the Horouta waka and, in 1769, the first European landfall in New Zealand.

name: Puhi Kai Iti / Cook Landing Site National Reserve
access_notes: Off Kaiti Beach Rd; small car park; lit sculptures visible at night
snippet: National historic reserve marking Cook's first landing on 8 October 1769 — dual Māori and European story told through contemporary sculpture; one of NZ's most significant historic sites.

name: Te Maro Sculpture
access_notes: Ruatanuika lookout on Titirangi; walk from Puhi Kai Iti or drive to upper car park
snippet: Striking 10 m perforated steel sculpture erected in 2019 at the lookout above Titirangi — commemorates Ngāti Oneone rangatira Te Maro, first Māori killed during Cook's 1769 landing.

name: Te Poho-o-Rāwiri Marae
access_notes: Queens Drive Kaiti; visible from road; visits by arrangement
snippet: Major Ngāti Oneone meeting house at the base of Titirangi — reputedly the largest carved meeting house in NZ when it opened in 1930; central to Gisborne's living Māori culture.

name: Captain Cook Monument & Young Nick Statue
access_notes: Tūranganui River Mouth Reserve; walkable from central Gisborne
snippet: Two monuments at the Tūranganui River mouth — Cook statue by Anthony Stones and Young Nick statue by Frank Szirmay, marking the site of NZ's first European landfall.

name: Gisborne Botanical Gardens
access_notes: Aberdeen Rd and Taruheru River; car park on Aberdeen Rd
snippet: Historic public gardens established in 1874 on the banks of the Taruheru River — 5.1 ha of mature trees including a century-old Moreton Bay fig, rose and cactus collections, aviary and playground.

name: Tūranganui River
access_notes: Walkable riverfront through central Gisborne; multiple access points
snippet: Reputedly the shortest river in the Southern Hemisphere at 1.2 km — the Tūranganui carries deep cultural significance as the site of Cook's first encounters with Māori in 1769.
